excerpt: Al-Kirmani teaches that the particular intellect (al-ʿaql al-juzʾī) deposited in the human soul is the inward correlate of the universal First Intellect and constitutes the natural disposition without which the acceptance of the prophetic sharīʿa would be impossible; the inward 'messenger' of reason prepares the soul for the outward Messenger of revelation.
source_location_hint: On the relation of the particular intellect to the universal intellect and to prophecy

excerpt: Al-Sijistani argues that human reason, left to its own resources, cannot rise of itself to the knowledge of God or of the Hereafter, and therefore stands in necessary need of the divinely-sent nāṭiq and his waṣī as the indispensable means of attaining religious truth.
source_location_hint: On the demonstration of prophecy from the limits of unaided intellect

excerpt: In his esoteric exegesis al-Qadi al-Nuʿman identifies ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib as the qasīm al-janna wa-l-nār, the divider of paradise and hellfire on the Day of Resurrection, through whom the believers receive the glad tidings (bishāra) and the deniers their warning (indhār).
source_location_hint: Taʾwīl of verses on bishāra and indhār in connection with the wilāya of ʿAlī

excerpt: Nāṣir-i Khusraw observes that the human child, isolated from human society, cannot acquire articulate speech although speech belongs to his nature; from this he infers that even the soul's natural endowments require a teacher, and a fortiori the knowledge of God requires a divinely-appointed instructor.
source_location_hint: On the necessity of a teacher (muʿallim) for the perfection of human nature

excerpt: Al-Qadi al-Nuʿman holds that the Qurʾān cannot interpret itself; its inward meanings (bāṭin) and its rulings require the waṣī of the Prophet as the authorised custodian of taʾwīl, without whom the community is left to follow conjecture and the lead of Satan.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, on the necessity of the Imam after the Prophet

excerpt: Al-Sijistani construes ayyām allāh as the cycles of the seven nāṭiqs together with their awṣiyāʾ, culminating in the Qāʾim al-qiyāma, in whose hand the final audit of souls will be effected.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the prophetic cycles and the Day of Resurrection

excerpt: The human being stands uniquely at the conjunction of sensibles (maḥsūsāt) and intelligibles (maʿqūlāt), composed of body and soul; this twofold composition is the very reason the human alone among creatures is the addressee of revelation and inner instruction.
source_location_hint: On the human as composed of sensibles and intelligibles, body and soul

excerpt: The taʾwīl of 'the Prophet is closer to the believers than their own selves, and his wives are their mothers' (Q 33:6) identifies, in the inner register, the Prophet as father of the believers and ʿAlī, his waṣī, as mother of the believers — the two principles whose conjunction generates the spiritual offspring of faith.
source_location_hint: Book of Walāya; taʾwīl of Q 33:6 on the Prophet's spiritual paternity

excerpt: Tanzīl is to taʾwīl as body to soul: the outward sharīʿa severed from its inner sense is a corpse, while taʾwīl without the works of the law is a disembodied spirit. The believer's salvation rests on the union of the two.
source_location_hint: On the body-and-soul figure of sharīʿa and taʾwīl

excerpt: Just as the body cannot live without nourishment from outside itself, the intellect — God's noblest gift to the human species — cannot ripen into knowledge without the teaching of one who already possesses it in its perfection. Divine wisdom requires that the bestower of intellect also provide its teacher.
source_location_hint: On the necessity of a divinely appointed teacher for the human intellect

excerpt: The Qurʾān's reference to revelation coming 'upon a man (rajul) from yourselves' (Q 7:63) is given in the singular: in every age there is one alone — Prophet, waṣī, or Imām — who is the warner and the dāʿī of his time; the office is never distributed across many.
source_location_hint: On the singular rajul as figure of the sole dāʿī of the age

excerpt: Every Imām is the khayr ('the best') of the community of his time, for he is its sole source of true knowledge and the criterion by which its right action is measured; the umma is called khayr umma only insofar as it follows him.
source_authority: Fatimid bāb and dāʿī al-duʿāt (5th/11th c.)
source_location_hint: On the superiority of the Imām over his community in the taʾwīl of Q 3:110

excerpt: On the day of Ghadīr Khumm, the Messenger of God took the hand of ʿAlī and proclaimed: 'Whomsoever I am his mawlā, this ʿAlī is his mawlā. O God, befriend whoever befriends him and oppose whoever opposes him.' This declaration, witnessed by the assembled community on its return from the Farewell Pilgrimage, constitutes the foundational naṣṣ upon which the imamate of the Commander of the Faithful and his descendants rests, and it is by virtue of this public affirmation that those who deny the designation are excluded from the rank of qualified witnesses.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qāḍī; foundational codification of Ismaili law and doctrine
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, opening chapters on the designation (naṣṣ) of the Imam

excerpt: The Imam is not constituted by the choice of the community, for were it so, his authority would derive from those who elected him, and the elector would in truth be more imam than the elected. The imamate, like prophethood, descends only by divine appointment transmitted through the preceding ḥujja, since obedience to the Imams is enjoined by the same divine command that enjoins obedience to the prophets, and what is established by God's authority cannot be undone by the deliberations of men.
source_authority: Tenth-century Fatimid-era Ismaili philosopher and dāʿī
source_location_hint: Sections on the necessity of the divinely appointed waṣī and the parallel between nubuwwa and imāma

excerpt: The naṣṣ does not terminate with a single generation: from the Messenger of God to ʿAlī, from ʿAlī to al-Ḥasan and al-Ḥusayn, then to ʿAlī Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn, then to Muḥammad al-Bāqir, then to Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq, and onward through his designated descendants, the imamate continues by explicit designation until the Day of Resurrection. No age is left without its Imam, just as no community of any prophet before was left without its appointed successor.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, chapters on the continuous chain of imāms after the Prophet

excerpt: Qadi al-Nuʿman opens the Kitāb al-Walāya by establishing that the Imam designated by God continues prophetic guidance in every age, citing Q 13:7 with Imam al-Bāqir's exegesis identifying the warner with the Prophet and the guide with ʿAlī and his descendants.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qadi under al-Muʿizz li-Dīn Allāh
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, opening chapters on the obligation of recognising the Imam

excerpt: Abū Yaʿqūb al-Sijistānī develops the polarity of the silent Book (al-Qurʾān al-ṣāmit) and its speaking exponent (al-Qurʾān al-nāṭiq), holding that the written muṣḥaf requires the living Imam for its taʾwīl to be unfolded.
source_location_hint: Sections on the relation of the nāṭiq, the asās, and the written revelation

excerpt: The Prophetic dictum 'whoever dies without knowing the Imam of his age dies the death of jāhiliyya' is transmitted by Qadi al-Nuʿman in the Daʿāʾim al-Islām as the master proof-text for the perpetual necessity of the Imamate after the closure of prophecy.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qadi under al-Muʿizz li-Dīn Allāh
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, on the obligation to recognise the Imam of one's age

excerpt: The Messenger of God explained to the people the manner of prayer, fasting, almsgiving, and pilgrimage, for these had been revealed in the Qur'an in summary, and he made manifest their detail.
source_authority: Qadi al-Nuʿman ibn Muhammad (d. 363/974), chief jurist of the Fatimid caliphate
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, opening sections on the obligation of recognizing the Imams; and Kitab al-Salat on the taṣliya formula

excerpt: Imam Jaʿfar al-Sadiq taught that the Family of Muhammad in the strict sense are the Ahl al-Bayt, while the believers who hold fast to their walāya are joined to them in the wider sense of religious affiliation.
source_authority: Qadi al-Nuʿman ibn Muhammad (d. 363/974), chief jurist of the Fatimid caliphate
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, traditions transmitted from Imam Jaʿfar al-Sadiq on the rank and definition of the Ahl al-Bayt

excerpt: Walāya is the binding pillar: the followed and the follower share a single destiny, whether the imam be one of guidance or one of misguidance.
source_authority: Qadi al-Nuʿman ibn Muhammad (d. 363/974), chief jurist of the Fatimid caliphate
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, on walāya as the foundation of the religion and the parallel structure of allegiance to imams of truth and imams of falsehood

excerpt: The Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt are the channels through which divine knowledge and grace descend to creation; they are the link between the higher pleroma and the world of religious community.
source_authority: Abu Yaʿqub al-Sijistani (d. after 361/971), leading Fatimid-era Ismaili daʿī and philosopher
source_location_hint: Sections on the necessity of the Imamate following prophecy and on the Imams as inheritors of the prophetic light

excerpt: Whoever recognises the Imam of his time and obeys him is counted among the believers; whoever divides God's command by professing belief in Him while rejecting His chosen has separated what God has joined.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, on the obligation of recognising the Imam

excerpt: The chain of the Imamate descends through the line of Ibrāhīm and Ismāʿīl to Muḥammad and ʿAlī, and from ʿAlī through Fāṭima to al-Ḥasan and al-Ḥusayn, continuing in their progeny without interruption.
source_location_hint: on the genealogical continuity of the Imamate

excerpt: The light of Muḥammad and ʿAlī was created before the rest of creation and transmitted through pure loins and chaste wombs, untouched by the defilement of jāhiliyya.
source_location_hint: on the prophetic light and its lineage of transmission

excerpt: The Imam is the comprehensive locus in whom the realities of the cosmos are gathered; what the macrocosm is to the world, the Imam is to humankind, and the human being is its lesser image.
source_location_hint: on the Imam as the comprehensive locus of the cosmic order

excerpt: He made those genera and species either a necessity for humans without which their physical existence and function would not be complete, or a source of nourishment, a medicine, an elegance, an adornment, and an indispensable tool for their existence.
source_location_hint: Discourse on the hierarchies of nature corresponding to the spiritual hudud

excerpt: Their nourishment is from the imam's knowledge, because the imams are the religious stars.
source_location_hint: Yanbu on the correspondence of celestial bodies to the ranks of the daʿwa

excerpt: Therefore, the essence that they describe, is a proof for the permanent, intellectual, luminous and authentic substance through which all souls are illumined and on which all nine depend.
source_location_hint: Treatment of jawhar in the philosophical sections on the Universal Intellect

excerpt: He forbade sadaqa to the Prophet so that they would not accuse him of taking them. Likewise, He forbade them to the Prophet's family so that they would know that the imam among them is the one who takes the position of the Prophet in collecting the alms.
source_location_hint: Books on zakat and khums

excerpt: The Commander of the Faithful, Ali Ibne Abu Talib, peace be upon him, said: Ask me before you lose me.
source_location_hint: Sermons of Imam Ali on his unrivalled knowledge
